html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 諧鐸 - A literary genre or collection of short stories or essays, often humorous or satirical.
  • 狐媚 - Literally 'fox charm' or 'fox seduction,' likely referring to a story or theme involving fox spirits, a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 虎痴 - Literally 'tiger madness' or 'tiger folly,' likely referring to a story or theme involving tigers.
  • 鸡谈 - Literally 'chicken talk,' likely referring to a story or theme involving chickens or possibly a metaphorical discussion.
  • 獭祭 - Literally 'beaver sacrifice,' likely referring to a story or theme involving beavers or a metaphorical act of sacrifice.
  • 蚁封 - Literally 'ant mound,' likely referring to a story or theme involving ants or a metaphorical structure.
  • 龟鉴 - Literally 'tortoise mirror,' likely referring to a story or theme involving tortoises or a metaphorical reflection.
  • 兔孕 - Literally 'rabbit pregnancy,' likely referring to a story or theme involving rabbits or a metaphorical conception.
  • 雉煤 - Literally 'pheasant coal,' likely referring to a story or theme involving pheasants or a metaphorical substance.
  • 屏角相郎 - Literally 'screen corner and young master,' likely referring to a story or theme involving a screen and a young man.
  • 笔头减寿 - Literally 'pen tip reduces lifespan,' likely referring to a story or theme involving writing and its consequences.
  • 讨猫檄 - Literally 'proclamation against cats,' likely referring to a story or theme involving a proclamation or declaration against cats.
  • 祭蠹文 - Literally 'text for sacrificing to bookworms,' likely referring to a story or theme involving a ritual or text dedicated to bookworms.
  • 隔牖谈诗 - Literally 'talking poetry through a window,' likely referring to a story or theme involving a conversation about poetry through a window.
  • 垂帘论曲 - Literally 'discussing opera behind a curtain,' likely referring to a story or theme involving a discussion of opera behind a curtain.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 諧鐸 - A genre of short stories or essays, often humorous or satirical, popular during the Qing dynasty.
  • 沈起鳳 - A Qing dynasty writer known for his works in this genre.
  • 狐媚 - Fox spirits are often depicted as seductive or mischievous in Chinese folklore.
  • 虎痴 - Tigers are often associated with strength and ferocity in Chinese culture, and 'tiger madness' could refer to a story where a tiger's behavior is erratic or unpredictable.
  • 鸡谈 - Chickens are often used in Chinese literature to symbolize simplicity or commonality.
  • 獭祭 - Beavers are known for their dam-building behavior, and 'beaver sacrifice' could be a metaphor for a significant act of construction or dedication.
  • 蚁封 - Ants are often used in Chinese literature to symbolize industriousness or small but significant actions.
  • 龟鉴 - Tortoises are often associated with longevity and wisdom in Chinese culture, and 'tortoise mirror' could be a metaphor for a reflective or wise perspective.
  • 兔孕 - Rabbits are often associated with fertility and new beginnings in Chinese culture.
  • 雉煤 - Pheasants are often used in Chinese literature to symbolize beauty or grace.
  • 屏角相郎 - Screens are often used in Chinese literature to symbolize privacy or separation, and 'young master' could refer to a noble or important figure.
  • 笔头减寿 - Writing is often associated with intellectual or spiritual pursuits in Chinese culture, and 'reducing lifespan' could be a metaphor for the sacrifices made in the pursuit of knowledge.
  • 讨猫檄 - Cats are often associated with independence and mystery in Chinese culture, and a 'proclamation against cats' could be a satirical or humorous piece.
  • 祭蠹文 - Bookworms are often used in Chinese literature to symbolize the dedication to learning, and a 'text for sacrificing to bookworms' could be a ritual or text dedicated to this dedication.
  • 隔牖谈诗 - Windows are often used in Chinese literature to symbolize a connection or separation between different worlds, and 'talking poetry through a window' could be a metaphor for a conversation that transcends physical boundaries.
  • 垂帘论曲 - Curtains are often used in Chinese literature to symbolize privacy or separation, and 'discussing opera behind a curtain' could be a metaphor for a private or intimate conversation.

Proper Nouns

  • 諧鐸 - The title of the work.
  • 沈起鳳 - The author's name.
  • 清 - The Qing dynasty.
  • 卷一 - The first volume or section of the work.
  • 卷二 - The second volume or section of the work.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 狐媚 - Compound term meaning 'fox charm' or 'fox seduction,' often used to describe deceptive or alluring behavior.
  • 性狷介 - Describes a person who is reclusive, aloof, and independent.
  • 日淫于书 - Literally 'drowning in books,' meaning to be deeply engrossed in reading.
  • 假园亭以憩 - Means to temporarily rest in a garden pavilion.
  • 假寐以伺 - Means to feign sleep while waiting or observing.
  • 名教中自有乐地 - Classical phrase meaning 'within the teachings of propriety, there is a place of joy,' suggesting that moral and social norms can be fulfilling.
  • 曳尾遁耳 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to drag one's tail and flee,' implying cowardice or retreat.
  • 田舍奴 - Derogatory term for a rustic or uneducated person.
  • 凭城作祟 - Means to cause mischief or harm within a city.
  • 假虎树威 - Means to borrow the power of a tiger to establish one's authority, implying deceit or flattery.
  • 祖德宗功 - Refers to the virtues and achievements of ancestors.
  • 绥绥庞庞 - Poetic expression describing a harmonious and prosperous family.
  • 昌都成室 - Means 'to thrive and build a home,' symbolizing success and stability.
  • 适文王遭羑里之囚 - Refers to the historical event where King Wen of Zhou was imprisoned in Youli.
  • 散宜生访先人于敝庐 - Means 'Shan Yisheng visited the ancestors in the humble dwelling,' referring to a historical episode.
  • 脱青翰以解之 - Means 'to release the blue phoenix to resolve the matter,' a metaphor for a diplomatic solution.
  • 篝灯弄谲 - Means 'to play tricks by lighting lamps,' implying deceitful behavior.
  • 卧榻宣淫 - Means 'to spread debauchery on the bed,' describing immoral or licentious acts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐媚 - The fox is a common symbol in Chinese folklore, often representing cunning, seduction, or transformation.
  • 名教 - Refers to the Confucian teachings of propriety and social order.
  • 大禹娶涂山之事 - Refers to the legendary story of Yu the Great marrying Lady Nüwa of the Tu Mountain clan, symbolizing the founding of a family and dynasty.
  • 文王遭羑里之囚 - Refers to the historical event where King Wen of Zhou was imprisoned in Youli, a pivotal moment in the Zhou dynasty's rise.
  • 散宜生访先人于敝庐 - Refers to the historical figure Shan Yisheng, who played a key role in King Wen's release from prison.
  • 脱青翰以解之 - Refers to the legendary blue phoenix, a symbol of auspiciousness and diplomatic resolution.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence lists the two ways foxes charm people: through beauty for those who are lustful, and through wealth for those who are greedy.
  • The first clause is a general statement about the joys of moral teachings, while the second is a specific critique of the man's narrow learning.
  • This sentence is a direct insult and warning, with the man calling the fox a 'wild fox' and accusing it of fleeing and speaking rashly.
  • This sentence combines a question about the man's knowledge with a historical reference to Yu the Great's marriage, linking it to ancestral virtue.
  • This long sentence recounts a historical event involving King Wen of Zhou and Shan Yisheng, linking it to the family's ancestral achievements.
  • The first clause acknowledges the historical truth, while the second criticizes the foxes' deceitful and immoral behavior.

Proper Nouns

  • 宁生 - The name of the main character, a reclusive scholar.
  • 大禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary Chinese leader and founder of the Xia dynasty.
  • 涂山 - The Tu Mountain, where Lady Nüwa is said to have lived.
  • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a key figure in the Zhou dynasty's rise.
  • 羑里 - Youli, the place where King Wen was imprisoned.
  • 散宜生 - Shan Yisheng, a historical figure who helped King Wen escape from prison.
  • 青翰 - The blue phoenix, a symbol of auspiciousness and diplomatic resolution.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 死则正邪 - Death is associated with moral concepts; the phrase suggests a connection between death and the balance of righteousness and evil.
  • 大圣犹羡其仁 - Even great sages admire their benevolence; this phrase highlights the admiration for virtue among wise men.
  • 穴则知雨 - Certain animals or phenomena can predict the weather; this is a metaphorical expression suggesting the ability to foresee natural events.
  • 汉儒尚钦其智 - Han scholars respected their wisdom; this indicates the admiration of Han scholars for the intelligence of the subject being discussed.
  • 有形九尾，德至乃来 - When virtue reaches its peak, mythical creatures like the nine-tailed fox appear; this suggests that the appearance of such creatures is a sign of high virtue.
  • 《山海》名经，言之凿凿 - The *Shanhai Jing* is a reliable source, clearly stating these facts; this emphasizes the credibility of the ancient text.
  • 始吾以汝等为不足齿之伧 - Originally, I considered you all as unworthy and vulgar; this phrase expresses initial disdain for the person being addressed.
  • 愿为书友 - I wish to be your scholarly friend; this indicates a desire to form a friendship based on shared scholarly interests.
  • 卿有意乎？请卜诸《易》 - Do you have an intention? Let us divine it using the *Yi Jing*; this suggests a request for divination to determine the other person's intentions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大圣 - Refers to great sages or wise men, often associated with Confucian ideals of virtue and wisdom.
  • 汉儒 - Scholars of the Han dynasty, known for their contributions to Confucian thought and the interpretation of classical texts.
  • 《周易》 - The *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes), an ancient Chinese divination text that is also a philosophical work, often used for guidance and insight.

Proper Nouns

  • 《周易》 - The *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes).

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 女随手占得‘末济’ - The woman casually drew the hexagram 'Wei Ji' (未濟). The hexagram 'Wei Ji' is the 64th in the *I Ching* (Book of Changes), symbolizing 'Not Yet Completed' or 'The Uncompleted.'
  • ‘未济’征凶，事不谐矣 - 'Wei Ji' signifies misfortune when moving forward, implying that the matter will not succeed. '征凶' means 'misfortune in movement' or 'adverse outcome if one proceeds.'
  • 小狐濡尾，虽不当位，刚柔应也，何害 - A line from the *I Ching* hexagram 'Wei Ji.' '小狐濡尾' (a small fox wets its tail) symbolizes a small misfortune. '虽不当位' means 'although it is not in the right position,' and '刚柔应也' means 'the interaction of strong and weak elements is appropriate,' suggesting that the misfortune is not severe.
  • 宁惑之，自此遂同寝处 - Ning was perplexed by it, and from then on, they lived together.
  • 不半月，神疲气殆，渐不可支 - Within half a month, spiritually exhausted and physically weak, gradually unable to bear it.
  • 友过而诘之，宁百方自讳 - A friend visited and questioned him, and Ning tried every means to conceal it.
  • 入夜女来，宁以病告 - At night the woman came, and Ning told her about his illness.
  • 文园善病，安知不因《封禅》一书 - Sima Xiangru, a famous writer from the Han dynasty known for his literary works and his frequent illnesses, how can we know it is not due to the book 'Fengchan' (封禅)?
  • 不然，茂陵姬且未聘，何由得消渴疾哉 - Otherwise, Sima Xiangru's wife, who had not yet been married, how could she have developed diabetes?
  • 日与女团坐一室 - He spent each day sitting together with the woman in the same room.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • ‘未济’ - The 64th hexagram of the *I Ching*, symbolizing 'Not Yet Completed' or 'The Uncompleted.' It often signifies a situation that is not yet resolved or completed.
  • 文园 - Refers to Sima Xiangru, a famous writer from the Han dynasty known for his literary works and his frequent illnesses.
  • 《封禅》一书 - Refers to the book 'Fengchan,' which is a historical record of the imperial rituals of Feng and Shan, performed by emperors to honor the gods.
  • 茂陵姬 - Refers to Sima Xiangru's wife, who had not yet been married, and is often associated with the story of Sima Xiangru's illness and his wife's devotion.
  • 消渴疾 - Refers to diabetes, a condition that was historically associated with excessive thirst and fatigue.

Proper Nouns

  • 宁 - The name of the male protagonist.
  • ‘未济’ - The 64th hexagram of the *I Ching*.
  • 文园 - Refers to Sima Xiangru.
  • 《封禅》一书 - Refers to the book 'Fengchan.'
  • 茂陵姬 - Refers to Sima Xiangru's wife.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又匝月 - Means 'another full month' or 'after another month,' indicating a passage of time.
  • 病体益深 - Means 'the illness became more severe,' describing the worsening condition of the protagonist.
  • 沉绵床褥 - Means 'lying motionless on the bed,' depicting the state of being bedridden.
  • 友复过之 - Means 'a friend visited again,' indicating a repeated visit.
  • 宁渐吐其实 - Means 'Ning gradually revealed the truth,' describing the protagonist's disclosure.
  • 君中媚人之上策矣 - Means 'this is the best strategy to flatter people,' referring to the protagonist's method of gaining favor.
  • 以色媚人者 - Means 'those who flatter with their beauty,' referring to women who use their appearance to gain favor.
  • 以金媚人者 - Means 'those who flatter with money,' referring to people who use wealth to gain favor.
  • 阳窃君子之行 - Means 'pretending to be a gentleman,' describing the protagonist's outward appearance of virtue.
  • 阴播小人之谲 - Means 'secretly spreading the tricks of a villain,' describing the protagonist's hidden deceit.
  • 择所好而投之 - Means 'choosing what the other person likes and offering it,' describing the protagonist's flattery.
  • 媚之术愈变 - Means 'the methods of flattery become more varied,' indicating the increasing sophistication of the protagonist's tactics.
  • 媚之毒愈长矣 - Means 'the poison of flattery becomes more lasting,' indicating the long-term negative effects of the protagonist's actions.
  • 宁戄然悔悟 - Means 'Ning suddenly realized and regretted,' describing the protagonist's sudden realization of his mistakes.
  • 友急唤舆人 - Means 'the friend quickly called for a sedan chair,' indicating the urgency of the situation.
  • 星夜舁归于家 - Means 'carried back home under the stars at night,' describing the hurried journey.
  • 女亦遂绝 - Means 'the girl also died,' indicating the tragic outcome.
  • 越半载 - Means 'after half a year,' indicating a passage of time.
  • 宁病瘵死 - Means 'Ning died of consumption,' describing the protagonist's death.
  • 遗书散佚 - Means 'the will was lost,' indicating the loss of important documents.
  • 后不可考 - Means 'and cannot be verified later,' indicating the uncertainty of the outcome.
  • 铎曰 - Means 'Duo said,' introducing a commentary.
  • 此朱门上客一面照心镜也 - Means 'this is a mirror reflecting the heart of a guest in a noble household,' describing the story as a reflection of human nature.
  • 打破天下人多少衣钵 - Means 'it shatters the beliefs and practices of many people,' indicating the story's impact.
  • 亦是我辈大罪过处 - Means 'it is also a great fault of our kind,' indicating the moral lesson.
  • 虎痴 - The title of the story, meaning 'Tiger Fool,' suggesting a tale of folly or misfortune.
  • 秦川女子霍小媖 - Refers to a woman named Huo Xiaoqing from the Qin region.
  • 有殊色 - Means 'with extraordinary beauty,' describing the woman's appearance.
  • 父与豪右某争田界 - Means 'her father had a dispute over land boundaries with a powerful local official,' describing the conflict.
  • 以他事诬诸官 - Means 'he was falsely accused of other matters by the officials,' indicating the injustice.
  • 竟毙于狱 - Means 'he was eventually killed in prison,' describing the tragic outcome.
  • 母痛哭曰 - Means 'the mother cried bitterly, saying,' introducing the mother's reaction.
  • 家无男子，谁为父复仇者 - Means 'there are no sons in the family, who will avenge my husband?' expressing the mother's despair.
  • 恐白骨冤埋，终作千秋黑狱矣 - Means 'I fear that my husband's bones will be buried in vain, and he will remain in eternal darkness forever,' expressing the mother's fear.
  • 女含涕而进曰 - Means 'the daughter, with tears in her eyes, stepped forward and said,' introducing the daughter's response.
  • 儿不肖，髫龄稚齿，不能作赵家娥 - Means 'I am unworthy, still young and inexperienced, but I cannot become a Zhao family girl,' expressing the daughter's self-deprecation.
  • 有得仇人而杀之者，儿愿执箕帚事之 - Means 'if anyone can kill the enemy, I will serve him as a servant,' expressing the daughter's determination.
  • 母鉴其诚，日以其言祷诸西山之麓 - Means 'the mother saw her sincerity and prayed to the gods on the western mountains every day,' describing the mother's actions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 朱门上客 - Refers to a guest in a noble household, often used to describe someone who is well-connected or influential.
  • 照心镜 - A metaphorical term for a mirror that reflects the true nature of one's heart, often used to describe a story or event that reveals the truth about someone's character.
  • 衣钵 - Literally 'robe and bowl,' a metaphor for the teachings or legacy passed down from a master to a disciple.
  • 虎痴 - The title of the story, meaning 'Tiger Fool,' suggests a tale of folly or misfortune.
  • 秦川 - Refers to the Qin region, a historical area in China known for its fertile land and strategic importance.
  • 霍小媖 - A fictional character in the story, representing a woman of extraordinary beauty and determination.
  • 豪右 - Refers to powerful local officials or landowners, often used to describe those with significant influence and wealth.
  • 赵家娥 - A reference to Zhao Feiyan, a famous courtesan in Chinese history known for her beauty and influence.
  • 西山 - Refers to the Western Mountains, a place often associated with religious or spiritual significance in Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 宁 - The protagonist of the first story.
  • 友 - The friend who visits the protagonist.
  • 铎 - The narrator or commentator of the first story.
  • 霍小媖 - The protagonist of the second story.
  • 父 - The father of Huo Xiaoqing.
  • 豪右 - The powerful local official who wronged Huo Xiaoqing's father.
  • 母 - The mother of Huo Xiaoqing.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日 - Time marker meaning 'one day' or 'on one day.'
  • 入城祝縣令壽 - Compound verb phrase meaning 'to enter the city to congratulate the county magistrate on his birthday.'
  • 路出西山 - Phrase meaning 'on the road out of the West Mountain.'
  • 虎突起於前 - Vivid expression meaning 'a tiger suddenly appeared in front of him.'
  • 啮喉而毙 - Phrase meaning 'bit his throat and killed him.'
  • 方额手庆 - Phrase meaning 'was just raising her hands in celebration.'
  • 曳尾而来 - Vivid expression meaning 'dragging its tail and came.'
  • 径登堂上 - Phrase meaning 'directly climbed onto the hall.'
  • 变色却走 - Phrase meaning 'changed color and retreated.'
  • 徘徊瞻眺 - Phrase meaning 'wandered around and looked around.'
  • 殊无恶意 - Phrase meaning 'showed no ill intent.'
  • 阖扉而语 - Phrase meaning 'closed the door and spoke.'
  • 雪我前仇 - Phrase meaning 'avenged my previous grudge.'
  • 茕茕母女 - Phrase meaning 'the lonely mother and daughter.'
  • 香花顶礼 - Phrase meaning 'worship with incense and flowers.'
  • 用酬大德 - Phrase meaning 'to repay great virtue.'
  • 降临玉趾 - Respectful phrase meaning 'descend your noble feet.'
  • 意欲何为 - Phrase meaning 'what do you intend to do?'
  • 怒目而视 - Phrase meaning 'glared with anger.'
  • 似憎其爽约者 - Phrase meaning 'seemed to hate those who broke their promise.'
  • 食言 - Phrase meaning 'to break one's word.'
  • 息壤在彼 - Phrase meaning 'the resting place is there.'
  • 本宜敬将幼女侍奉裳衣 - Phrase meaning 'it would have been appropriate to respectfully serve the young girl with clothing.'
  • 起居寝食 - Phrase meaning 'daily life and eating.'
  • 彼此道殊 - Phrase meaning 'our ways are different.'
  • 安得竟成伉俪 - Phrase meaning 'how could we ever become husband and wife?'
  • 倚婿为活 - Phrase meaning 'rely on the son-in-law for support.'
  • 地下人报怨 - Phrase meaning 'vengeance from the dead.'
  • 未亡人施德 - Phrase meaning 'to bestow kindness on the widow.'
  • 陈衷曲 - Phrase meaning 'express my sincere feelings.'
  • 乞赐矜全 - Phrase meaning 'ask for your mercy and forgiveness.'
  • 颔之 - Phrase meaning 'nodded in agreement.'
  • 嘉纳 - Phrase meaning 'accepted gladly.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 县令 - Refers to the county magistrate, a local government official.
  • 西山 - Refers to the West Mountain, a geographical location.
  • 虎 - Symbolizes danger and wildness in Chinese culture.
  • 香花顶礼 - A traditional practice of worship using incense and flowers.

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 女乃导虎入帷 - Literally means 'the woman led the tiger into the tent.' Emphasizes the unusual and intimate relationship between the woman and the tiger.
  • 营菟裘于绣榻之旁 - Literally means 'she prepared a rabbit fur robe beside the embroidered bed.' Suggests a luxurious and comfortable setting.
  • 食则同牢，居则同室 - Literally means 'they ate from the same sacrificial vessel and lived in the same room.' Indicates a deep bond and shared life.
  • 侧目偷窥 - Literally means 'peering sideways.' Describes the tiger's cautious and attentive behavior.
  • 竟夕不寐 - Literally means 'stayed awake all night.' Highlights the tiger's vigilance and care.
  • 恐以鼾声扰其清梦也 - Literally means 'afraid that snoring would disturb her clear dreams.' Shows the tiger's consideration for the woman's comfort.
  • 衔鹿脯以进 - Literally means 'brought deer jerky.' Indicates the tiger's provision of food.
  • 抱小恙，焦思躁急，盘旋室内者无停趾 - Literally means 'when she had a slight illness, she was anxious and restless, roaming inside the room without stopping.' Describes the woman's emotional state and the tiger's constant presence.
  • 病愈，始欢跃如初 - Literally means 'when she recovered, she rejoiced as before.' Shows the restoration of their relationship.
  • 母氏因年迈无依，时咎女之失计，而遇虎礼貌亦衰 - Literally means 'her mother, being old and without support, often blamed the woman for her poor judgment, and her treatment of the tiger also became less respectful.' Reveals the mother's disapproval.
  • 虎一夕竟去 - Literally means 'one night, the tiger suddenly left.' Marks a significant event in the story.
  • 执不允 - Literally means 'she refused steadfastly.' Shows the woman's determination.
  • 后女以郁疾死，停尸堂上 - Literally means 'later, the woman died of melancholy and was laid out in the hall.' Marks the tragic end of the story.
  • 虎忽嗥哭而来，泪下如雨，进殓者皆见之 - Literally means 'the tiger suddenly howled and wept, tears streaming down, those who came to view the body all saw it.' Vividly describes the tiger's grief.
  • 继埋玉于祖茔之侧，虎一日巡视者三 - Literally means 'then she was buried beside the ancestral tomb, and the tiger visited three times a day.' Shows the tiger's continued devotion.
  • 春秋令节，辄衔山果以奠 - Literally means 'on the four seasonal festivals, the tiger would bring mountain fruits to pay respects.' Highlights the tiger's ongoing remembrance.
  • 越三载如一日 - Literally means 'for three years, as if it were one day.' Emphasizes the consistency of the tiger's behavior.
  • 母贫乏不能自话，虎犹日取山獐野兔，存恤其家云 - Literally means 'the mother, being poor and unable to speak for herself, the tiger still brought mountain deer and wild rabbits to support her family.' Shows the tiger's continued care for the family.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 女 - Refers to a woman, likely a courtesan or a person of low social status.
  • 虎 - Refers to a tiger, a symbol of strength and loyalty in Chinese culture.
  • 帷 - Refers to a tent or curtain, indicating a private and intimate space.
  • 绣榻 - Refers to an embroidered bed, suggesting luxury and comfort.
  • 同牢 - Refers to eating from the same sacrificial vessel, a ritual act symbolizing unity and commitment.
  • 同室 - Refers to living in the same room, indicating a close and shared life.
  • 清梦 - Refers to clear dreams, emphasizing the woman's peaceful state.
  • 鹿脯 - Refers to dried deer meat, a common food item.
  • 小恙 - Refers to a slight illness, indicating a minor health issue.
  • 焦思躁急 - Refers to being anxious and restless, describing the woman's emotional state.
  • 盘旋室内者无停趾 - Refers to roaming inside the room without stopping, emphasizing the tiger's constant presence.
  • 病愈，始欢跃如初 - Refers to recovering and rejoicing as before, showing the restoration of their relationship.
  • 母氏 - Refers to the woman's mother, indicating a family relationship.
  • 失计 - Refers to poor judgment, reflecting the mother's disapproval.
  • 背德不祥，负恩非福 - Refers to moral principles, emphasizing the importance of virtue and gratitude.
  • 形骸之论 - Refers to physical form, contrasting with the emotional bond.
  • 郁疾 - Refers to melancholy or depression, indicating a mental health issue.
  • 停尸堂上 - Refers to laying out the body in the hall, a common practice in traditional Chinese funerals.
  • 虎忽嗥哭而来，泪下如雨 - Refers to the tiger's emotional response, showing its deep bond with the woman.
  • 进殓者皆见之 - Refers to those who came to view the body, indicating the public nature of the event.
  • 埋玉 - Refers to burying, often used metaphorically for death.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 前果后猎，左倪右若，皆龟之体也 - Describes the turtle's body in a playful, metaphorical way, where '前果后猎' suggests a rounded shape, '左倪右若' implies a balanced symmetry, and '皆龟之体也' confirms that it is the body of a turtle.
  • 谈相，偶然适中 - Refers to discussing physiognomy (the art of judging character by facial features) and accidentally hitting the mark.
  • 软火囤 - A playful term for a person who, after successfully predicting something, acts like a turtle with its head up and tail wagging, feeling pleased with themselves.
  • 便好休 - A term for a person who, after failing to predict something, acts like a turtle retracting its head and tail, quietly retreating.
  • 结二十八宿之党，用七十二钻之技 - Refers to forming alliances with the 28 constellations and using the 72 drilling techniques, likely a metaphor for mastering various skills or methods.
  • 六眸尽瞎，四足犹忙 - A metaphorical expression suggesting that despite having six eyes (implying many perspectives), they are all blind, but the four feet (actions) are still busy.
  • 龟俯者有灵，遇忌我者必鞠躬 - A turtle that bows has spirit; when it meets someone who dislikes it, it will bow respectfully.
  • 龟寝者无息，遇骂我者且忍气 - A sleeping turtle does not breathe; when it is insulted, it will endure the anger.
  • 风鉴之道行矣 - The way of physiognomy is now practiced.
  • 尔等盍以龟鉴 - You all should use the turtle as a mirror.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 龟 - In Chinese culture, turtles are often associated with longevity, wisdom, and good fortune. They are also symbols of stability and resilience.
  • 风鉴 - Refers to the art of physiognomy, which is the practice of assessing a person's character or future based on their physical appearance.
  • 二十八宿 - The 28 constellations, an ancient Chinese system of dividing the sky into 28 segments.
  • 七十二钻 - Likely refers to a set of techniques or skills, possibly related to divination or craftsmanship.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '前果后猎，左倪右若，皆龟之体也' describes the turtle's body in a playful, metaphorical way, which can be challenging to translate directly.
  • The phrase '偶然适中' can be ambiguous, as it could mean 'accidentally hit the mark' or 'happened to be right.'
  • The parallel structure of the sentence '结二十八宿之党，用七十二钻之技' can be complex to translate while maintaining the metaphorical meaning.
  • The metaphorical expression '六眸尽瞎，四足犹忙' can be challenging to convey in English, as it involves a play on words and imagery.
  • The conditional structure and the metaphorical meaning of '龟俯者有灵，遇忌我者必鞠躬' can be difficult to capture in English.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening metaphor '予临尔众，慷乎若铁索之驭六马' describes the speaker's commanding presence and must be read as a single, vivid image.
  • The long sequence of vices '内作盗荒，外作淫荒，甘懒嗜顽，钻穴逾墙' must be read as a single, comprehensive list.
  • The proverbial expression '有一于此，未有不亡' emphasizes the inevitability of decline and must be read as a single, forceful statement.
  • The formal expression '惟我高堂，有此义方' asserts moral authority and must be read as a single, authoritative statement.
  • The formal expression '汝悖厥训，乱其纪纲，乃底灭亡' warns of the consequences of disobedience and must be read as a single, cautionary statement.
  • The formal expression '巍巍我主，一家之尊' praises the ruler and must be read as a single, reverent statement.
  • The formal expression '有礼有法，贻厥后人' emphasizes the importance of tradition and must be read as a single, directive statement.
  • The formal expression '吟诗诵赋，昔人则有' highlights cultural heritage and must be read as a single, reflective statement.
  • The formal expression '荒坠厥绪，诲淫绝耻' criticizes moral decay and must be read as a single, condemnatory statement.
  • The formal expression '呜呼急归，予怀之悲' expresses concern and must be read as a single, emotional statement.
  • The formal expression '人实诳女，女将畴依' warns of betrayal and must be read as a single, cautionary statement.
  • The formal expression '郁陶乎予心，颇厚有忸怩' expresses inner turmoil and must be read as a single, introspective statement.
  • The formal expression '苟悔厥过，来者可追' offers hope for redemption and must be read as a single, hopeful statement.
  • The formal expression '从巧思慧舌，大率类是' concludes the speech and must be read as a single, summarizing statement.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 捕鼠将佛奴者 - Metaphorical phrase where '佛奴' (Buddha's slave) refers to someone who pretends to be virtuous but is actually weak and cowardly.
  • 性成巽懦，貌托仁慈 - Describes a person who is naturally timid and weak but pretends to be kind and merciful.
  • 学雪衣娘之诵经，冒尾君子之守矩 - Metaphorical phrase where '雪衣娘' (Snow-Cloaked Lady) refers to someone who pretends to be virtuous by reciting scriptures, while '冒尾君子' (Tail-wagging Gentleman) refers to someone who pretends to follow the rules.
  • 花阴昼懒，不管翻盆，竹簟宵慵，由他凿壁 - Describes a lazy and indifferent attitude, where the person is indifferent to the chaos around them.
  • 九子环魔母之宫，叠辈登肩，六贼戏弥陀之崖 - Metaphorical phrase where '九子' (nine sons) and '六贼' (six thieves) refer to a chaotic and disorderly group, while '魔母之宫' (Mother of Demons' Palace) and '弥陀之崖' (Amitabha's Cliff) are symbolic places.
  • 傀儡登场，无声无臭 - Metaphorical phrase where '傀儡' (puppet) refers to someone who is manipulated and controlled, and '无声无臭' (silent and odorless) describes their lack of impact or influence.
  • 优柔寡断，姑息养奸 - Describes a person who is indecisive and indulges in wrongdoing, allowing evil to flourish.
  • 阎罗怕鬼，扫尽威风，大将怯兵，丧其纪律 - Metaphorical phrase where '阎罗' (Yama, the god of death) and '大将' (general) refer to powerful figures who lose their authority and discipline.
  • 自甘唾面，实为纵恶之尤，谁生厉阶，尽出沽名之辈 - Describes a person who willingly accepts humiliation and is the worst kind of evil-doer, while '厉阶' (stairway to disaster) and '沽名' (seeking fame) refer to the causes of disaster and those who seek fame.
  • 排楚人犬牙之阵，整蔡州骡子之军 - Metaphorical phrase where '楚人犬牙之阵' (Chu dogs' array) and '蔡州骡子之军' (Cai state mule army) refer to a chaotic and disorganized force.
  • 悬诺狐首竿头，留作前车之鉴；缚向麒麟檀上，且观后效之图 - Metaphorical phrase where '狐首' (fox head) and '麒麟' (qilin, a mythical creature) refer to the consequences of one's actions, and '前车之鉴' (lesson from a previous failure) and '后效之图' (plan for future consequences) refer to the lessons and consequences to be learned.
  • 共奋虎威，勿教兔脱 - Metaphorical phrase where '虎威' (tiger's might) and '兔脱' (rabbit escaping) refer to the need to act decisively to prevent escape.

  • 体卑质丧 - Means 'the form is low and the substance is lost,' criticizing the decline in poetic quality during the Liang and Chen Dynasties.

  • 近体以何为宗 - Means 'what is the model for regulated verse?' referring to the classical style of Tang poetry.

  • 终日不言不笑 - Means 'remaining silent and without laughter all day.'
  • 似学天竺菩提九年面壁者 - 似学 means 'as if learning,' 天竺 is an ancient name for India, 菩提 refers to enlightenment, and 九年面壁 means 'nine years of facing the wall' (a reference to the Buddhist monk Bodhidharma).
  • 蒲团未破，红粉先埋 - 蒲团 refers to a meditation cushion, 未破 means 'not broken,' 红粉 is a poetic term for a woman, and 先埋 means 'buried first.'
  • 岂导师之诳语乎 - 岂 means 'is it,' 导师 means 'teacher' or 'guru,' and 诳语 means 'false words.'
  • 抑金棺双足，将现迦叶身而得度也 - 抑 means 'or,' 金棺 refers to a golden coffin, 双足 means 'both feet,' 迦叶 refers to Kāśyapa, a disciple of the Buddha, and 得度 means 'to attain enlightenment.'
  • 姑记之，与叶小鸾参禅一案，并为词坛佳话云 - 姑记之 means 'let's record it,' 与 means 'with,' 叶小鸾 is likely a person's name, 参禅 means 'to meditate,' 一案 means 'a case' or 'an incident,' 并为 means 'together with,' 词坛 means 'literary world,' and 佳话 means 'a good story.'
  • 铎曰 - 铎 is a surname, and 曰 means 'says.'
  • 昔五祖以袈裟度世 - 昔 means 'in the past,' 五祖 refers to the Fifth Patriarch of Chan Buddhism, 以 means 'with,' 袈裟 means 'monastic robe,' and 度世 means 'to save the world.'
  • 于五百人中，必择一钝汉予之 - 于 means 'among,' 五百人 means 'five hundred people,' 必择 means 'must choose,' 一钝汉 means 'a dull person,' and 予之 means 'give it to him.'
  • 乃知金莲法界，非聪明人插脚地也 - 乃知 means 'thus know,' 金莲法界 refers to the realm of the Golden Lotus, 非 means 'not,' 聪明人 means 'clever people,' and 插脚地 means 'a place to stand.'
  • 我辈欲参大乘，惟愿生生世世，勿作有情之物 - 我辈 means 'we,' 欲参大乘 means 'want to practice the Mahayana path,' 惟愿 means 'only wish,' 生生世世 means 'in countless lives,' and 勿作有情之物 means 'not to be a sentient being.'
  • 穷士扶乩 - 穷士 means 'a poor scholar,' and 扶乩 means 'to perform a divination ritual.'
  • 吴中马颠，能诗，工词曲，而名不山里巷 - 吴中 refers to Wu region, 马颠 is a person's name, 能诗 means 'can write poetry,' 工词曲 means 'skilled in lyrics and music,' and 而名不山里巷 means 'but his name is not known in the countryside.'
  • 饥驱潦倒，薄游于扬 - 饥驱潦倒 means 'driven by hunger and poverty,' and 薄游于扬 means 'traveling lightly in Yangzhou.'
  • 以诗遍谒贵游，三载卒无所遇 - 以诗遍谒贵游 means 'to visit all the wealthy and noble with poetry,' and 三载卒无所遇 means 'after three years, still without meeting anyone.'
  • 适虹桥荷花盛开，鹾贾设宴园亭，招名士之客于扬者 - 适 means 'just as,' 虹桥 refers to a bridge, 荷花盛开 means 'lotus flowers blooming,' 鹾贾 means 'salt merchant,' 设宴园亭 means 'hosting a banquet in the garden pavilion,' and 招名士之客于扬者 means 'inviting famous scholars from Yangzhou.'
  • 马私挟诗稿而往，阍人阻之，马排闼直入 - 马私挟诗稿而往 means 'Ma privately took his poems to go,' 阍人阻之 means 'the gatekeeper stopped him,' and 马排闼直入 means 'Ma pushed the door open and entered directly.'
  • 时扬州扶乩正盛，就近地借得沙盘等具，排列中庭 - 时扬州扶乩正盛 means 'at that time, divination was very popular in Yangzhou,' 就近地借得沙盘等具 means 'borrowed the sand tray and other equipment nearby,' and 排列中庭 means 'arranged in the courtyard.'

  • 死便埋我 - Metaphorical expression; '死便埋我' (if I die, bury me) expresses a resigned or fatalistic attitude, suggesting that death is inevitable and there is no point in trying to change one's fate.
  • 作达者亦何益哉 - Metaphorical expression; '作达者' (acting as a great man) and '何益哉' (what good is it) question the value of trying to achieve greatness or recognition, implying that such efforts are futile.
  • 取金留犁、玉蟾蜍 - Metaphorical expression; '取金留犁' (taking gold and leaving the plow) refers to abandoning one's duties or responsibilities for wealth, while '玉蟾蜍' (jade toad) is a symbol of luxury or excess. Together, the phrase criticizes the pursuit of material wealth at the expense of one's duties.
  • 尽以西江水涤之 - Metaphorical expression; '尽以西江水涤之' (wash it all away with water from the Western River) is a metaphor for cleansing or purifying something, often used in the context of washing away sins or impurities.
  • 醒狂论一则 - Metaphorical term; '醒狂论' (a single wakeful madman's argument) refers to a brief, perhaps eccentric or unconventional statement or opinion.
  • 从此塞瓿覆瓮可也 - Metaphorical expression; '从此塞瓿覆瓮' (from now on, seal the jars and cover the pots) is a metaphor for ending a practice or tradition, suggesting that it is time to stop or abandon something.
  • 桓温在座，日给二升 - Historical reference; '桓温' (Huan Wen) was a famous general and politician of the Eastern Jin Dynasty. '日给二升' (daily allowance of two liters) refers to a daily ration of wine, suggesting that Huan Wen was known for his indulgence in wine.
  • 景伯登筵，礼严三爵 - Historical reference; '景伯' (Jing Bo) was a historical figure known for his strict adherence to ritual and propriety. '礼严三爵' (strictly adhering to the three cups of wine) suggests that Jing Bo was known for his strict adherence to ritual and propriety in drinking.
  • 入非麴友，路入糟邱 - Metaphorical expression; '入非麴友' (entering not a friend of wine) and '路入糟邱' (the road leads to a place of fermentation) describe entering a world of indulgence or debauchery.
  • 喜则芗泽迷心 - Metaphorical expression; '喜则芗泽迷心' (when happy, the fragrance of incense confuses the heart) describes a state of intoxication or distraction caused by pleasure or indulgence.
  • 淳于髡合樽错坐 - Historical reference; '淳于髡' (Chunyu Kun) was a famous jester and philosopher of the Warring States period. '合樽错坐' (drinking together in disorder) suggests a chaotic or unrestrained drinking session.
  • 怒则车轮括颈 - Metaphorical expression; '怒则车轮括颈' (when angry, the wheel of the carriage binds the neck) is a metaphor for being overwhelmed by anger or frustration.
  • 高季式恃势留宾 - Historical reference; '高季式' (Gao Jishi) was a historical figure known for his arrogance and use of power. '恃势留宾' (using power to detain guests) suggests that Gao Jishi was known for using his authority to hold onto his guests or associates.
  • 酣态凌人 - Metaphorical expression; '酣态凌人' (the state of intoxication overpowers others) describes a person who is so drunk or intoxicated that they dominate or intimidate others.
  • 醉乡狎色 - Metaphorical expression; '醉乡狎色' (in the world of drunkenness, familiar with beauty) describes a state of indulgence in both alcohol and physical pleasure.
  • 渴老羌于池畔 - Metaphorical expression; '渴老羌于池畔' (thirsty Qiang people by the pond) is a metaphor for a situation where people are in dire need or distress.
  • 缚以投池 - Metaphorical expression; '缚以投池' (tie up and throw into the pond) is a metaphor for punishing or abandoning someone.
  • 桎毕卓于瓮边 - Metaphorical expression; '桎毕卓于瓮边' (shackle Bi Zuo at the edge of the jar) is a metaphor for restraining or controlling someone who is indulging in excess.
  • 请其入瓮 - Metaphorical expression; '请其入瓮' (ask him to enter the jar) is a metaphor for punishing someone by forcing them to face the consequences of their actions.

  • 淫人妻者，妻亦得淫人报 - Moralistic statement meaning 'those who defile others' wives will also receive defilement in return,' emphasizing the principle of retribution.
  • 奸与杀近 - Phrase meaning 'adultery is close to murder,' suggesting a moral equivalence or progression from one to the other.
  • 可长以身试乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'can one continue to test this with one's own life?' implying a warning against repeated transgression.
  • 归家后，与妻颇敦琴瑟 - Poetic expression meaning 'after returning home, he became very close with his wife,' using the metaphor of a harmonious musical instrument to describe marital harmony.
  • 倡楼伎馆中，亦杳无某生迹矣 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'in brothels and entertainment houses, there is no trace of the person anymore,' indicating a change in behavior or disappearance from such places.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 淫人妻 - Refers to the act of committing adultery, which was considered a serious moral offense in traditional Chinese society.
  • 琴瑟 - Metaphor for marital harmony, derived from the ancient Chinese musical instruments *qin* (琴) and *se* (瑟).
  • 倡楼伎馆 - Refers to brothels and entertainment houses, which were common in traditional Chinese cities and often associated with moral decline.
  • 某生 - Reference to the person being described, whose identity is not specified.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 客馆宣淫，深闺揖盗 - Metaphorical expression. '客馆宣淫' means 'promiscuity in guesthouses,' and '深闺揖盗' means 'welcoming bandits into the inner chambers.' Together, they suggest that indulgence in vice invites disaster.
  • 现在盘珠，不劳头上翁覆算也 - Figurative phrase. '盘珠' likely refers to 'round pearls' or 'round coins,' symbolizing wealth. '头上翁覆算' means 'the old man on one's head will take care of the calculation,' implying that one should not worry about financial matters.
  • 戒之！戒之！ - Repeated exhortation, emphasizing the need for caution or warning.
  • 出乎尔者，反乎尔者也 - Proverb meaning 'what you do to others will come back to you,' a form of the Golden Rule.
  • 墙茨难扫，即以此言，作千金敝帚也可 - Metaphorical statement. '墙茨难扫' means 'moss on the wall is hard to sweep away,' symbolizing persistent problems. '作千金敝帚' means 'make a broom worth a thousand gold pieces,' suggesting that even something worthless can be valuable if treated properly.
  • 越此疆而侵彼界，必至爱野鹜而失家鸡 - Metaphorical warning. '越此疆而侵彼界' means 'crossing boundaries,' and '爱野鹜而失家鸡' means 'loving wild ducks while losing domestic chickens,' suggesting that overreaching can lead to loss.
  • 天道好还，人言可畏 - Proverbs meaning 'heaven rewards good and punishes evil' and 'people's words can be feared,' respectively.
  • 须知此日宣淫榻上，即是插标卖妇之媒，岂待他年诲盗闺中，始悟反火焚身之渐！ - Complex metaphorical statement. '插标卖妇之媒' means 'a signpost for selling a wife,' and '反火焚身之渐' means 'the gradual self-immolation by fire,' suggesting that current indulgence will lead to future ruin.
  • 日颠倒四时花木以为戏 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'he plays with flowers and trees, reversing the seasons.'
  • 适成连子迟予海上，当暂去，明晨复来 - Narrative phrase. '适成连子迟予海上' means 'just as Cheng Lianzi delayed me by the sea,' and '当暂去，明晨复来' means 'I must leave temporarily and return tomorrow morning.'
  • 此物有福则享，无福则殃，尔等勿轻觑也 - Cautionary statement. '此物有福则享，无福则殃' means 'this object brings good fortune if you have it, misfortune if you don't,' and '尔等勿轻觑也' means 'do not underestimate it.'

  • 五岳子 - Self-proclaimed title meaning 'Son of the Five Great Mountains,' often used by wandering scholars or hermits.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 受業許元凱附識 - Title indicating an appendix to the teachings of Xu Yuankai; '受業' means to receive instruction, '許元凱' is the teacher's name, and '附識' means appendix or additional note.
  • 氣戒 - Combines '氣' (qi, breath or spirit) and '戒' (admonition or warning), suggesting a warning about the influence of emotions or spirits.
  • 甫周岁 - '甫' means 'just' or 'barely,' and '周岁' means 'one year old,' indicating the child is just one year old.
  • 捉其子付庄抱之 - '捉其子' means to take her child, '付庄' means to give to Zhuang, and '抱之' means to hold it, describing the act of handing the child to Zhuang.
  • 再四谆嘱 - '再四' means repeatedly or many times, and '谆嘱' means to give earnest instructions, indicating that the instructions were given repeatedly and earnestly.
  • 登舆而去 - '登舆' means to get into the carriage, and '而去' means and leave, describing the act of getting into the carriage and departing.
  • 拍案而怒 - '拍案' means to bang the table, and '而怒' means and be angry, describing the act of banging the table in anger.
  • 拙哉祖龙 - '拙哉' means how foolish, and '祖龙' is a respectful term for the First Emperor of Qin, Qin Shi Huang, expressing disapproval of Qin Shi Huang's actions.
  • 尔欲尽愚黔首 - '尔欲' means you want, '尽愚' means to make completely stupid, and '黔首' refers to common people, criticizing Qin Shi Huang for trying to make the common people stupid.
  • 琅玡记德碑教谁识也 - '琅玡' refers to the Lángyá stele, '记德碑' means stele of merit, and '教谁识也' means who will recognize it, questioning the purpose of the stele.
  • 博浪沙锥击 - '博浪沙' refers to the location of a famous assassination attempt, and '锥击' means to strike with a dagger, referring to the assassination attempt on Qin Shi Huang.
  • 惜哉！天不绝秦，副车仅中 - '惜哉' means alas, '天不绝秦' means Heaven did not abandon Qin, '副车' means secondary carriage, and '仅中' means just barely hit, expressing regret that the assassination attempt failed.
  • 否则鲍鱼遗臭，何俟三十六年后哉 - '否则' means otherwise, '鲍鱼遗臭' means the stench of a rotten fish, '何俟' means why wait, and '三十六年后' means thirty-six years later, suggesting that if the assassination had succeeded, the stench of Qin's tyranny would not have lasted so long.
  • 沛公入关，鸿门掷斗 - '沛公' refers to Liu Bang, the founder of the Han dynasty, '入关' means to enter the pass, '鸿门' refers to the Hongmen Feast, and '掷斗' means to throw down the goblet, referring to Liu Bang's escape from the Hongmen Feast.
  • 勃然大怒，拍案起立 - '勃然大怒' means to become extremely angry, '拍案起立' means to bang the table and stand up, describing Zhuang's reaction to Liu Bang's escape.
  • 此时纵却，后将奈何 - '此时' means at this time, '纵却' means even if they retreat, '后将奈何' means what will happen afterward, questioning what would happen if Liu Bang had retreated.
  • 不识亚父计，老重瞳当抉去矣 - '不识' means do not understand, '亚父' refers to Xiang Yu's advisor, Fan Zeng, '老重瞳' refers to Xiang Yu, and '当抉去矣' means should have been removed, criticizing Fan Zeng for not recognizing the need to remove Xiang Yu.
  • 烹翁鼎上，分我杯羹 - '烹翁鼎上' means to cook on the cauldron, '分我杯羹' means to share my bowl of soup, referring to Liu Bang's promise to share power with his allies.
  • 怒气填胸，翻案而起 - '怒气填胸' means anger fills the chest, '翻案而起' means to overturn the table and stand up, describing Zhuang's intense anger.
  • 父子如此，君臣可知 - '父子如此' means parents act like this, '君臣可知' means how can ministers know, criticizing the relationship between Liu Bang and his father.
  • 走狗之烹，夫何怪哉 - '走狗之烹' means to cook the hunting dog, '夫何怪哉' means what is strange about that, suggesting that it is not surprising that Liu Bang betrayed his father.
  • 面青气塞，不复作啼声矣 - '面青气塞' means the face turned blue and the breath was blocked, '不复作啼声矣' means no longer made any crying sounds, describing the child's condition after Zhuang's outburst.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 耿氏 - Refers to Zhuang's second wife, who is described as loving her son very much.

  • 然犬马毙，亦当埋帷盖 - But when the dog or horse dies, it should also be buried with a canopy, indicating a respectful burial for animals.
  • 苟假尺土而掩之，实君之盛德 - If even a small piece of land is provided to cover it, it is truly a great virtue of the lord, suggesting that providing a burial place for the dog is an act of virtue.
  • 乘间谓仆曰 - Taking the opportunity to tell the servant, indicating a moment of private conversation.
  • 尔知我心愿否 - Do you know my wish?, indicating a direct question about the servant's understanding.
  • 我乞人妻耳，骤作富家妇，饮食起居，都不惯 - I am just a beggar's wife, suddenly becoming a wealthy family's wife, I am not used to the food, clothing, and daily life, indicating the woman's discomfort with her new status.
  • 但得如尔者事之，则我愿足矣 - If I can have someone like you to serve me, then my wish will be fulfilled, indicating the woman's desire for a simple and honest relationship.
  • 奈主人何 - What about the master?, indicating a concern about the master's reaction.
  • 是不难 - It is not difficult, indicating that the plan is feasible.
  • 急首于官 - Quickly report to the authorities, indicating a plan to involve the law.
  • 则主人必系缧绁中 - Then the master will definitely be imprisoned, indicating the expected outcome of reporting to the authorities.
  • 尔与我席卷而遁 - You and I will flee together, indicating a plan to escape.
  • 向他乡作一小贸易 - Go to another place and engage in a small trade, indicating a plan for a new life.
  • 差胜低头檐下也 - It is better than bowing one's head under the eaves, indicating a preference for independence over servitude.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天报 - Refers to the concept of divine retribution, a common theme in Chinese literature and philosophy.
  • 乞人妻 - Refers to a woman from a low social status, specifically a beggar's wife.
  • 富家妇 - Refers to a woman from a wealthy family, indicating a significant social change.
  • 系缧绁 - Refers to being imprisoned, a common punishment in ancient China.
  • 席卷而遁 - Refers to a dramatic and secretive escape, often used in stories of rebellion or escape.

  • 我乞人妻耳，骤作富家妇，饮食起居，都不惯 - This sentence is a detailed description of the woman's discomfort with her new status.

  • 窜豁谷间 - Means 'running into a valley,' suggesting a sudden or unexpected change in circumstances.

  • 刺足钩衣 - Means 'pricking feet and snagging clothes,' describing the discomfort and inconvenience of navigating through thorny terrain.
  • 若不觉其苦 - Means 'as if they do not feel the pain,' suggesting a lack of awareness or concern about the difficulties they face.
  • 摄衣欲入 - Means 'pulling up one's clothes and wanting to enter,' indicating a readiness or determination to face a challenging situation.
  • 健步 - Means 'strong steps' or 'robust strides,' often used to describe someone who is physically fit or confident.
  • 窜入荆棘 - Means 'running into thorns,' suggesting a reckless or misguided action.
  • 颠其趾 - Means 'to twist their toes,' indicating a minor but significant setback or injury.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 钱神 - A term often used in classical Chinese literature to personify money as a powerful and manipulative force.
  • 造化 - Refers to fate or natural order, often used in the context of destiny or divine will.
  • 嘉耦 - A term commonly used in classical Chinese literature to describe a harmonious and excellent partnership, often in the context of marriage.
  • 荆棘里 - A metaphorical term used to describe a difficult or dangerous situation, often associated with moral or ethical challenges.

  • 有遗书寄女。女偕卢迹至尼庵，见床头横禅杖一枝，犹是昔年枪杆也。 - This sentence describes the woman's journey to find her mother. It means 'She left a letter for the daughter. The woman traveled with Lu to find the temple, saw a single horizontal Zen staff on the bed, which was still the same as the old spear shaft.'
  • 女与卢皆大哭，瘗其柩于东山之阳，庐墓三年，然后同返。 - This sentence describes the woman's mourning and return. It means 'The woman and Lu both wept greatly, buried her coffin on the sunny side of East Mountain, lived in a hut by the grave for three years, and then returned together.'
  • 铎曰：“天之所福，慈孝为先。女知爱母，故不作覆巢之卵，母知爱女，故不作断颈之凫。独是溺于女者，何以不从厥夫？哀其母者，何以不及其父？君子曰，‘此其所以为盗也。’嗟乎，世之不为盗者多矣，而盗且然乎？” - This sentence is a commentary by the narrator. It means 'The bell said, 'Heaven's blessings start with filial piety and kindness. The daughter knows how to love her mother, so she does not become an egg in a overturned nest, the mother knows how to love her daughter, so she does not become a swan with a severed neck. Those who are devoted to their daughters, why do they not follow their husbands? Those who mourn their mothers, why do they not mourn their fathers? The gentleman said, 'This is why they become thieves.' Alas, there are many in the world who do not become thieves, and even thieves are like this.'

  • 砉然一声，身裂为两，饱啖之 - With a loud sound, his body split in two, and he was devoured, a dramatic and violent description of the punishment.

  • The criticism in '汝一市井无赖子，幸乃兄以草鞋起家，即当恪遵家法，守汝敝屣' is harsh, but the exact context and relationship between the characters are not clear.
  • The accusation in '辄敢盔我名器，假冒士林，宣淫闺闼' is severe, but the exact context and relationship between the characters are not clear.
  • The rhetorical question in '罪何可恕' emphasizes the severity of the offense, but the exact context and relationship between the characters are not clear.

  • The sarcastic remark in '如此一双泥腿，消受得几许福分' is clear, but the exact context and relationship between the characters are not clear.
  • The violent description in '砉然一声，身裂为两，饱啖之' is dramatic, but the exact context and relationship between the characters are not clear.
  • The decisive action in '提靴竟去' is clear, but the exact context and relationship between the characters are not clear.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 女惊绝，半日而苏 - Literal description of a woman being shocked to the point of fainting and then regaining consciousness after half a day.
  • 后适里中某氏子 - Formal expression meaning 'later she married a certain man from the village.'
  • 殆有覆车之鉴耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'perhaps as a warning against a similar disaster?'
  • 白丁爱着皂靴 - Literal description of an uneducated person enjoying wearing black boots.
  • 牛蹄犬爪，何处消此罪过 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'how can the sins of a cow's hoof and a dog's paw be atoned for?'
  • 幸钟先生长守后户 - Formal expression meaning 'fortunately, Master Zhong has long guarded the rear door.'
  • 不然，登堂入室，不居然履声橐橐哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'otherwise, when entering the hall and the inner chamber, wouldn't the sound of footsteps be clearly heard?'
  • 江以南多健讼者，而吴下为最 - Formal expression meaning 'southern Jiang has many litigious people, and Wu is the most so.'
  • 性贪黠，善作讼词 - Literal description of a person being greedy and cunning, skilled in writing legal documents.
  • 梦鬼役押赴阎罗殿 - Literal description of a dream where a person is led by ghostly officials to the hall of Yama.
  • 王凭案先鞫其父 - Formal expression meaning 'the king, leaning on the desk, first interrogated his father.'
  • 士、农、工、商，各有恒业 - Formal expression meaning 'scholars, farmers, artisans, and merchants, each have their own constant occupations.'
  • 人以金帛啖我，姑却之，而目眈眈出火，不得已诺之 - Literal description of a person being tempted by gold and silk, initially refusing but then succumbing to the temptation.
  • 王曰：“尔等挟何术，能颠倒黑白若此？” - Formal expression meaning 'the king said, 'What tricks do you use to make black and white seem reversed?''
  • 柳下惠坐怀，作强奸论 - Historical reference to Laozi Xi, who was known for his virtue. The phrase '作强奸论' means to fabricate a charge of rape.
  • 管夷吾受骈邑，可按侵夺田产律也 - Historical reference to Guan Zhong, who was known for his administrative skills. The phrase '可按侵夺田产律也' means to apply the law on the seizure of property.
  • 是则诬直为曲矣！而拗曲作直则何如？ - Formal expression meaning 'this is to accuse the innocent of guilt! And what about twisting the truth to make it seem right?'
  • 傲象杀兄，是遵父命；陈平盔嫂，可曰援溺也 - Historical references to Oao Xiang and Chen Ping. The phrase '傲象杀兄，是遵父命' means 'Oao Xiang killed his brother, following his father's command,' and '陈平盔嫂，可曰援溺也' means 'Chen Ping helped his sister-in-law, which can be called saving someone from drowning.'

  • 大步而前，仿佛运粮河漕船过闸也 - She walked with large strides, as if a grain transport boat was passing through a lock; '仿佛' means as if or like.

  • 名士操三寸管，驰骋词坛 - Scholars wield their three-inch brushes, galloping across the literary arena; '操' means to wield, '三寸管' refers to a brush, and '驰骋' means to gallop or roam freely.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 伸手过膝者必大贵 - Traditional belief that a person whose arm can reach their knee is destined for great wealth or status.
  • 咄咄！傀儡，是大贵神 - Rhetorical exclamation expressing skepticism or mockery, comparing the person to a puppet and questioning their supposed greatness.
  • 旋过学署 - "旋过" means "to pass by" or "to go through," and "学署" refers to an educational institution or academy.
  • 不识也 - Literally "did not recognize," used here to indicate misunderstanding or lack of knowledge.
  • 竞摩其顶 - "竞" means "to compete" or "to vie," and "摩其顶" means "to rub the top of its head," implying a playful or mocking gesture.
  • 汝前生负何重孽 - "汝" means "you," "前生" means "past life," "负" means "to bear," and "重孽" means "heavy sin," suggesting a belief in karma or past-life consequences.
  • 今向人前出丑若是 - "今向人前" means "now in front of people," and "出丑" means "to disgrace oneself," indicating embarrassment or shame.
  • 大笑而去 - Literally "laughed loudly and left," describing a dismissive or mocking departure.
  • 值母诞辰 - "值" means "on the occasion of," "母诞辰" means "mother's birthday," referring to a family celebration.
  • 夜演《鸿门宴》杂剧 - "夜演" means "performed at night," "《鸿门宴》" is the title of a historical play about the Feast at Hong Gate, and "杂剧" means "miscellaneous drama" or "variety show."
  • 群客在座 - "群客" means "many guests," and "在座" means "present" or "seated," indicating a gathering of people.
  • 忽场上樊哙提刀直前 - "忽" means "suddenly," "场上" means "on stage," "樊哙" is the name of a historical figure, "提刀" means "to lift a knife," and "直前" means "straight ahead," describing a dramatic entrance.
  • 主宾尽失色 - "主宾" means "host and guests," and "尽失色" means "all turned pale," indicating fear or shock.
  • 我赑屃神也 - "我" means "I," "赑屃" is the name of a mythical creature, and "神也" means "a god," claiming divine identity.
  • 本为龙子 - "本为" means "originally was," "龙子" means "son of a dragon," indicating a noble or supernatural origin.
  • 上帝怜我有勇无文 - "上帝" means "God" or "Heaven," "怜" means "pities," "有勇无文" means "brave but lacking in culture," suggesting a need for improvement.
  • 故令负石学宫 - "故令" means "therefore ordered," "负石" means "to carry stones," and "学宫" means "academy" or "school," indicating a punishment or task.
  • 稍窥文墨 - "稍" means "slightly," "窥" means "to glimpse," and "文墨" means "writing and learning," suggesting a limited exposure to education.
  • 不幸负形蠢坌 - "不幸" means "unfortunately," "负形" means "having a form," "蠢坌" means "stupid and crude," indicating a perceived flaw.
  • 贼奴误认为龟 - "贼奴" means "servant" or "slave," "误认为" means "mistakenly thought," and "龟" means "tortoise," indicating a misunderstanding.
  • 妄加姗笑 - "妄加" means "arbitrarily," "姗笑" means "mocking laughter," indicating disrespectful behavior.
  • 汝一市井无赖 - "汝" means "you," "市井" means "marketplace" or "common people," and "无赖" means "rascal" or "scoundrel," indicating a derogatory label.
  • 平日帷簿不修 - "平日" means "usually," "帷簿" means "morals and conduct," and "不修" means "not maintained," indicating poor behavior.
  • 吃（食追）子亦醉 - "吃" means "to eat," "（食追）子" is a typo or unclear phrase, possibly referring to "food" or "eating," and "亦醉" means "also drunk," indicating a state of intoxication.
  • 真所谓神似非形似者 - "真所谓" means "truly what is called," "神似" means "spiritually similar," and "非形似" means "not physically similar," indicating a deeper meaning.
  • 乃不自量 - "乃" means "thus," "不自量" means "does not measure up," indicating a lack of self-awareness.
  • 反谓予人前出丑 - "反" means "instead," "谓" means "to say," "予" means "to give," and "人前出丑" means "to disgrace oneself in front of others," indicating a reversal of roles.
  • 今日贺客满堂 - "今日" means "today," "贺客" means "guests of celebration," and "满堂" means "filling the room," indicating a festive atmosphere.
  • 且与尔折证此案 - "且" means "and," "与尔" means "with you," "折证" means "to argue or debate," and "此案" means "this case," indicating a dispute.
  • 提刀欲杀 - "提刀" means "to lift a knife," and "欲杀" means "to want to kill," indicating a threatening action.
  • 匍伏乞命 - "匍伏" means "to crawl," and "乞命" means "to beg for one's life," indicating a plea for mercy.
  • 客亦代为哀救 - "客" means "guests," "亦" means "also," "代为" means "on behalf of," and "哀救" means "to plead for mercy," indicating support.
  • 因掷刀而笑曰 - "因" means "then," "掷刀" means "to throw down the knife," and "而笑曰" means "and said with a laugh," indicating a change in mood.
  • 留骨而贵 - "留骨" means "to preserve one's bones," and "而贵" means "and become noble," indicating a desire for honor.
  • 宁其生而曳尾于涂也 - "宁" means "rather than," "其生" means "its life," "曳尾" means "dragging its tail," and "于涂" means "on the road," indicating a preference for a humble existence.
  • 姑赦之 - "姑" means "temporarily," "赦" means "to forgive," and "之" means "it," indicating a temporary pardon.
  • 撒手登场 - "撒手" means "to let go," and "登场" means "to appear on stage," indicating a return to normalcy.
  • 仍演《鸿门》剧本 - "仍" means "still," "演" means "to perform," "《鸿门》" is the title of the play, and "剧本" means "script," indicating a continuation of the performance.
  • 依然一樊哙耳 - "依然" means "still," "一" means "a," "樊哙" is the name of the historical figure, and "耳" means "only," indicating a return to normalcy.
  • 问之，亦不省 - "问之" means "asked him," and "亦不省" means "also did not understand," indicating confusion.
  • 吴下喧传其事 - "吴下" means "in Wu," "喧传" means "widely spread," and "其事" means "the story," indicating a rumor.
  • 遂置某于不齿 - "遂" means "therefore," "置" means "to place," "某" means "a certain person," and "于不齿" means "on the list of disreputable people," indicating social ostracism.
  • 后两弟兄援例入监 - "后" means "later," "两弟兄" means "the two brothers," "援例" means "by example," and "入监" means "to enter prison," indicating a legal consequence.
  • 人犹呼为“衣锦荣龟”云 - "人" means "people," "犹" means "still," "呼为" means "call as," "衣锦荣龟" means "clothed in brocade and honored as a tortoise," and "云" means "so it is said," indicating a nickname.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 伸手过膝者必大贵 - Traditional belief in Chinese culture that a person with long arms is destined for greatness.
  • 《鸿门宴》 - Title of a historical play about the Feast at Hong Gate, a significant event in Chinese history.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 人之多言，亦可畏也 - Proverbial expression meaning 'Many words, even if they are just talk, can be feared.' It suggests that idle chatter or gossip can have serious consequences.
  • 然未免谑而虐矣 - Compound expression meaning 'but it cannot help being jesting and cruel.' It implies that the situation, while potentially harmful, also has elements of humor or cruelty.
  • 因书片札 - Phrase meaning 'wrote a short note' or 'left a brief message.'
  • 命狄赴龙虎山，乞天师治之 - Phrase meaning 'ordered Di to go to Longhu Mountain to ask the Celestial Master to deal with it.'
  • 至则门庭宏敞，宫殿森严 - Phrase meaning 'Upon arrival, the courtyard was spacious and the palaces were imposing.'
  • 处处悬牌，书神将名守护 - Phrase meaning 'Flags hung everywhere, inscribed with the names of divine generals for protection.'
  • 司阍者入内启禀 - Phrase meaning 'The gatekeeper went inside to report.'
  • 约两时许，召狄进见 - Phrase meaning 'After about two hours, Di was summoned to meet.'
  • 众法官拥天师出 - Phrase meaning 'The judges escorted the Celestial Master out.'
  • 虎皮椅坐莲华帐中 - Phrase meaning 'Sitting on a tiger skin chair in a lotus canopy.'
  • 金印宝剑，陈列法座 - Phrase meaning 'Gold seal and precious sword displayed on the altar.'
  • 狄匍伏檐下，呈状法官 - Phrase meaning 'Di knelt at the eaves and presented the case to the judge.'
  • 法官转呈座上 - Phrase meaning 'The judge then presented it to the Celestial Master.'
  • 天师细阅一过，摇首攒眉，沉吟良久 - Phrase meaning 'The Celestial Master carefully read it, shook his head, furrowed his brow, and pondered for a long time.'
  • 与法官耳语，不知作何词 - Phrase meaning 'Whispered something to the judge, but it is unknown what was said.'
  • 即以片纸付狄，令上清官道人作法 - Phrase meaning 'Then gave a piece of paper to Di and instructed the Daoist at the Upper Pure Palace to perform a ritual.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 龙虎山 - Refers to Longhu Mountain, a famous Taoist mountain in Jiangxi Province, known for its association with the Celestial Masters of the Zhengyi Dao tradition.
  • 天师 - Refers to the Celestial Master, a title given to the head of the Zhengyi Dao tradition.
  • 上清官 - Refers to the Upper Pure Palace, a Taoist temple or institution.
  • 吴郡 - Refers to Wu Prefecture, an ancient administrative region in present-day Jiangsu Province.
  • 三茅观 - Refers to the Sanmao Temple, a Taoist temple dedicated to the Three Sovereigns.
  • 司马 - Can be a surname or a title, referring to a high-ranking official in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 龙虎山 - Longhu Mountain.
  • 天师 - Celestial Master.
  • 上清官 - Upper Pure Palace.
  • 吴郡 - Wu Prefecture.
  • 三茅观 - Sanmao Temple.
  • 司马 - Surname or title.

Idiomatic Expressions

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 而遭人终不可见，遂叹息而反 - Narrative phrase indicating encountering something unseen and sighing in disappointment.

  • 人乃于富贵中求奇士，是犹向莲华帐底买符箓也 - Metaphorical statement criticizing the search for extraordinary individuals among the wealthy.
  • 森罗殿点鬼 - Reference to the Hall of Judgment in the underworld, where souls are judged.
  • 荷枷带锁辈，由东廊鱼贯而进 - Vivid description of condemned souls entering the hall, symbolized by people carrying shackles and chains.
  • 唱名毕，偃蹇从西廊出 - Narrative phrase describing how the names of the condemned are called and they leave the hall dispiritedly.
  • 继点勾魂簿，唱名再四，无一人应者 - Statement indicating that when the names on the soul-calling register are called, no one responds.
  • 奉后殿转轮王命，俾男者为医，女者为妓，尽托生人世矣 - Explanation that the souls of men have been sent to become doctors and women to become courtesans, all reborn in the human world.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 行未数武 - Literally 'after walking a few steps,' used to indicate a short distance.
  • 值旧识 - Means 'encountering an old acquaintance.'
  • 强邀过寓 - Means 'strongly invited to visit his residence.'
  • 甫登堂 - Means 'as soon as he entered the hall.'
  • 两鬟堆茉莉如雪 - Describes the hairstyle as piled with jasmine flowers like snow.
  • 着蝉翼衫 - Means 'wearing a thin, gauzy shirt.'
  • 左右袒露 - Means 'exposing both sides of the body.'
  • 红墙一抹 - Describes a red wall as a slight touch or hint.
  • 下曳冰绡裤 - Means 'wearing a silk skirt that drapes down.'
  • 白足拖八寸计蝴蝶履 - Describes the feet as bare, wearing butterfly-shaped shoes about eight inches long.
  • 不甚酬接 - Means 'not very responsive or engaged.'
  • 摩两臂金条脱铮然作响 - Means 'rubbing her gold bracelets on her arms, making a clinking sound.'

  • 黄曰 - Means 'Huang said.'
  • 此秦淮文状元某姬也 - Means 'This is a certain lady who was the literary champion of the Qinhuai River.'

  • 果是不凡 - Means 'indeed is extraordinary.'
  • 司空见惯 - Means 'commonplace and seen often.'
  • 仆不能向石榴裙底攀高谒贵 - Means 'I cannot grovel at the feet of such a lady.'
  • 匆匆告别 - Means 'hurriedly bid farewell.'
  • 急欲回寓 - Means 'eagerly wanted to return to his lodging.'
  • 查曰 - Means 'Zha said.'
  • 未到桃源，何言返桌 - Means 'Not having reached Peach Blossom Spring, how can you talk about returning?'
  • 刘愤然曰 - Means 'Liu angrily said.'
  • 状元若此，探花可知 - Means 'If the状元 is like this, what about the探花 (second-place winner)?'
  • 吾宁识英雄于孙山之外 - Means 'I would rather recognize heroes beyond the ranks of the successful.'
  • 不敢向及第花下抡才矣 - Means 'I dare not seek talent under the flowers of the successful.'
  • 拂袖竟归 - Means 'waved his sleeves and returned.'
  • 查述诸小三 - Means 'Zha recounted the story of the third person.'
  • 俯首不语 - Means 'lowered his head and remained silent.'
  • 既而叹曰 - Means 'afterward sighed and said.'
  • 前明复社诸君 - Refers to the members of the Revival Society during the Ming Dynasty.
  • 中周延儒榜进士 - Means 'passed the imperial examination under the list of Zhou Yanru.'
  • 比诸佛头着粪 - Means 'compared to defecating on the head of a Buddha.'
  • 儿不幸与若辈联名 - Means 'I am unfortunate to be associated with such people.'
  • 宜为英流唾弃也 - Means 'should be spurned by the noble and talented.'
  • 抚床一恸 - Means 'threw himself on the bed and wept bitterly.'
  • 潸潸泪下 - Means 'tears streamed down.'
  • 查劝慰，乃止 - Means 'Zha comforted him, and he stopped.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦淮文状元 - Refers to a literary champion from the Qinhuai River area, known for its cultural significance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The first line is a metaphorical expression, while the second line is a statement of beauty. The relationship between the two lines is that the breaking of the hairpin (a symbol of something precious) does not diminish the beauty of the brocade.
  • The first line describes the intimate relationship between lovers, while the second line suggests that the speaker is confiding their feelings to a symbolic figure of love. The two lines are connected by the theme of love and intimacy.
  • The first line refers to the speaker's literary talent, while the second line describes the beloved's beauty and spiritual elevation. The two lines are connected by the theme of the couple's complementary qualities.
  • The first line suggests that the couple will serve together in a high-ranking position, while the second line indicates that they have been separated for a long time. The two lines are connected by the theme of the couple's journey together.
  • The first line suggests that the couple's journey is confined to the mortal world, while the second line describes the beloved's beauty. The two lines are connected by the theme of the couple's temporary existence.
  • These three lines describe a progression from indulgence to a more meaningful pursuit. The first line refers to drinking in the past, the second line to seeking pleasure in the present, and the third line to acquiring a mountain in the future.
  • The first line suggests that the couple will live in seclusion together, while the second line describes their harmonious and eternal union. The two lines are connected by the theme of the couple's future together.

  • 媒妁之言 - Refers to a marriage arranged by matchmakers, a traditional Chinese practice.
  • 急脉而缓受之，全失命题之旨矣 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that one should respond to urgency with urgency, or else miss the point. '全失命题之旨矣' means 'completely misses the intent of the topic.'

  • 读书人最易昧心 - '昧心' means to act against one's conscience or moral principles.
  • 一朝倍师，保不作逢蒙杀羿乎 - '倍师' means to betray a teacher, and '逢蒙杀羿' refers to the myth of Feng Meng killing Yi, a legendary archer, symbolizing betrayal.
  • 指誓山河，矢盟日月 - Means to swear by mountains and rivers, and to pledge one's oath by the sun and moon, indicating a solemn promise.

  • 结褵之夕 - '结褵' refers to the wedding night, '褵' being a type of garment worn by the bride.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes the protagonist's daily routine, with '日則' and '夜則' indicating a temporal contrast between day and night. The phrase '如依老僧座下' is a metaphorical expression that requires careful rendering to preserve its spiritual connotation.
  • This sentence indicates that the protagonist passed the provincial examination and then returned to the temple, but the connection between these two events is implied rather than explicitly stated.
  • This sentence describes a sequence of events: the monk closes the door as before, the protagonist passes the metropolitan examination, becomes an official in the Hanlin Academy, and then serves as the chief examiner in Hubei. The temporal and causal relationships between these events are implied and require careful reconstruction in English.
  • This sentence contrasts the protagonist's luxurious life with his spiritual practice, with '入則玉堂，出則绛帳' describing his material status and '蒲團佛火，未嘗一日不在夢寐中' describing his spiritual devotion. The juxtaposition of these two aspects requires careful rendering to preserve the balance between material and spiritual life.
  • This sentence describes the passage of time and the protagonist's decision to retire. The phrase '荏冉十二年' is a poetic expression that conveys the slow passage of time, and '屈指舊約' suggests a sense of fulfillment or completion of a long-term commitment.

  • 空空妙手少年场 - Phrase meaning 'the empty-handed wondrous hands of the young.' '空空' (empty-handed) suggests lack of resources or connections, and '妙手' (wondrous hands) implies skill or talent, while '少年场' (young field) refers to the arena of youth or early career.
  • 莫赋《高轩过》 - Reference to not composing a poem in the style of 'Gao Xuan Guo,' a famous poem by Li Bai, which describes a high carriage passing by. Here, it suggests avoiding grand or overly ambitious writing.
  • 防却明珠失锦囊 - Metaphorical warning to 'guard against losing a pearl in a brocade bag,' meaning to protect valuable things from being lost or misused.
  • 予不能诗，亦不至落魄乃尔也 - Humble statement meaning 'I cannot compose poetry, nor have I fallen to such a state.' '落魄' (fallen to ruin) implies a state of poverty or disgrace.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 五祀之内，门居其首 - Classical phrase meaning 'Among the five sacrifices, the sacrifice to the gate is the first.' '五祀' refers to the five sacrificial rites, and '门' specifically denotes the gate.
  • 馁鬼处处觅食 - Literally 'hungry ghosts seek food everywhere,' but used metaphorically to describe restless spirits wandering in search of sustenance.
  • 遂于白昼现将军丞相形矣 - Literally 'they thus appear as generals and chancellors during the day,' but used to describe how these spirits manifest in human form, often in positions of authority.
  • 何牛班头之神，尚穿黑直身哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why does the deity who presides over cattle still wear a black robe?' Challenges the logic of the deity's attire.
  • 或曰：‘冠进贤，系羽箭者，是其变相耳！’ - Quote from someone suggesting that 'those who wear the hat of a scholar and carry feathered arrows are merely its transformed appearance.' Implies that the deity's appearance has changed over time.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五祀 - Refers to the five sacrificial rites in ancient Chinese religion: the sacrifice to the gate, the stove, the well, the land, and the millstone.
  • 门 - The gate is one of the five sacrificial rites, symbolizing the threshold between the human and spiritual worlds.
  • 馁鬼 - Hungry ghosts, a concept from Chinese folk religion, representing spirits that wander without rest due to unfulfilled desires or obligations.
  • 将军丞相 - Generals and chancellors, high-ranking officials in ancient Chinese government, often used metaphorically to describe powerful or authoritative figures.
  • 牛班头 - The deity who presides over cattle, often associated with agricultural and pastoral activities.
  • 冠进贤，系羽箭 - The hat of a scholar (进贤冠) and carrying feathered arrows, symbols of scholarly and military status, respectively.

  • 牛班头 - The deity who presides over cattle.

  • 排偶 - Refers to parallel or paired phrases, often used in classical Chinese literature.

  • 耀婺墨于东壁，降王母于西池 - '耀婺墨' means 'shining like the star of the North Star,' '东壁' means 'eastern wall,' '降王母' means 'descending the Queen Mother,' '西池' means 'western pool.' Together, it means 'shining like the North Star on the eastern wall, descending the Queen Mother to the western pool.'
  • 巾帼增辉，璇闺益寿 - '巾帼' means 'women's headgear' or 'women,' '增辉' means 'adding luster,' '璇闺' means 'jade chamber' or 'woman's quarters,' '益寿' means 'increasing longevity.' Together, it means 'adding luster to women's headgear, increasing longevity in the jade chamber.'
  • 堪喷饭 - Means 'laugh so hard that one spits out food,' indicating something extremely funny or absurd.
  • 赍送 - Means 'to carry and deliver' or 'to present.'
  • 一书记耳 - '一书记' means 'a clerk' or 'a secretary,' '耳' is a particle meaning 'only' or 'just.'
  • 浙中名士 - '浙中' means 'in Zhejiang,' '名士' means 'famous scholar' or 'distinguished person.'
  • 戟门揖客 - '戟门' means 'gate with spears' or 'gate of a high-ranking official,' '揖客' means 'to greet guests.' Together, it means 'to greet guests at the gate with spears.'
  • 所获当有什倍于此者 - '所获' means 'what is gained,' '当' means 'should,' '有' means 'have,' '什倍' means 'ten times,' '于此' means 'compared to this.' Together, it means 'what is gained should be ten times more than this.'
  • 托故辞去 - '托故' means 'to make an excuse,' '辞去' means 'to leave' or 'to resign.' Together, it means 'to leave by making an excuse.'
  • 诣抚辕 - '诣' means 'to go to,' '抚辕' means 'the governor's office.' Together, it means 'to go to the governor's office.'
  • 投揭求见 - '投揭' means 'to submit a petition,' '求见' means 'to request an audience.' Together, it means 'to submit a petition and request an audience.'
  • 知己之感 - '知己' means 'close friend' or 'confidant,' '感' means 'feeling' or 'gratitude.' Together, it means 'the feeling of gratitude to a close friend.'
  • 察抚公意似不甚招接者 - '察' means 'to observe,' '抚公' means 'the governor,' '意' means 'intention,' '似' means 'seems,' '不甚' means 'not very,' '招接' means 'to receive.' Together, it means 'observed that the governor's intention seems not very welcoming.'
  • 申言某县令寿章，系某代撰 - '申言' means 'to state,' '某县令' means 'a certain county magistrate,' '寿章' means 'longevity article,' '系' means 'is,' '某' means 'a certain person,' '代撰' means 'written on behalf of.' Together, it means 'stated that the longevity article for the county magistrate was written on behalf of a certain person.'
  • 大窘而退 - '大窘' means 'greatly embarrassed,' '而退' means 'and withdrew.' Together, it means 'greatly embarrassed and withdrew.'
  • 群呼为巾帼幕宾 - '群呼' means 'called by the crowd,' '为' means 'as,' '巾帼幕宾' means 'female guest scholar.' Together, it means 'called him a female guest scholar.'
  • 到处求荐，卒无有聘之者 - '到处' means 'everywhere,' '求荐' means 'to seek recommendations,' '卒' means 'finally,' '无有' means 'no one,' '聘之者' means 'who hires him.' Together, it means 'sought recommendations everywhere, but finally no one hired him.'

  • 急诣鸨母家 - '急诣' means 'hurriedly went to,' and '鸨母' refers to a brothel owner or madam.
  • 玉蕊得生死耗，绝粒者三日 - '得生死耗' means 'received news of life and death,' and '绝粒者三日' means 'stopped eating for three days.'
  • 生吐其实，皆大喜 - '吐其实' means 'revealed the truth,' and '皆大喜' means 'all were greatly pleased.'
  • 遂以金聘之而归 - '遂以金聘之' means 'then paid with gold to hire her,' and '而归' means 'and returned home.'
  • 因感兰蕊德，移其柩礼葬之 - '因感兰蕊德' means 'because of gratitude for Lan Rui's virtue,' and '移其柩礼葬之' means 'moved her coffin to bury her with proper rites.'
  • 后葛氏子孙繁衍，命春秋祭扫，永着为例 - '后葛氏子孙繁衍' means 'later, the descendants of the Ge family multiplied,' '命春秋祭扫' means 'ordered to perform sacrifices in spring and autumn,' and '永着为例' means 'perpetually established as an example.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 葛氏 - Refers to the Ge family, a family name.
  • 鸨母 - A brothel owner or madam, indicating a social context related to the sex industry.
  • 不动尊 - A statue of a Buddha or bodhisattva, suggesting a religious or spiritual context.
  • 兰蕊 - Likely a person's name, possibly a courtesan or a woman of virtue.
  • 玉蕊 - Another person's name, likely referring to a different individual.
  • 春秋祭扫 - A traditional Chinese custom of performing sacrifices and cleaning graves in spring and autumn.

Proper Nouns

  • 葛氏 - The Ge family.
  • 鸨母 - A brothel owner or madam.
  • 不动尊 - A statue of a Buddha or bodhisattva.
  • 兰蕊 - A person's name, possibly a courtesan or a woman of virtue.
  • 玉蕊 - Another person's name.

[214]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 十斛量珠 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'ten hu of pearls,' signifying an enormous amount of wealth or treasure.
  • 千里结网 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to weave a net over a thousand miles,' symbolizing extensive planning or a vast network.
  • 家无黄金屋 - Literally 'no golden house in the home,' a reference to the lack of material wealth, often alluding to the famous line from the *Book of Songs*.
  • 阿娇从何处贮哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'where would Lady A-Jiao store herself?' Lady A-Jiao is a reference to a concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, symbolizing luxury and indulgence.
  • 温柔乡里 - Metaphor for a place of comfort and indulgence, often associated with the pursuit of pleasure.
  • 坑煞几多寒士 - Literally 'how many cold scholars have been trapped,' a lament for those who have been ensnared by the pursuit of pleasure or wealth.
  • 欲海沉身 - Metaphor for being overwhelmed by desires, often leading to one's downfall.
  • 泉台埋骨 - Euphemism for death, referring to being buried in the underworld.
  • 鬼门关外 - Metaphor for the afterlife or the threshold of death.
  • 独立茫茫 - Literally 'standing alone in the vast emptiness,' a description of isolation and desolation.
  • 元宝汤向谁家吃也 - Rhetorical question meaning 'who will eat the money soup?' a sarcastic remark about the futility of pursuing wealth.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 十姨庙 - Temple dedicated to the Ten Aunts, a group of deities or spirits worshipped in Chinese folk religion.
  • 杜曲西 - Location in the western part of Duzhi, an area in ancient China.
  • 芝楣桂栋 - Ornate architectural features, with '芝楣' (orchid beams) and '桂栋' (osmanthus pillars) symbolizing elegance and refinement.
  • 椒壁兰帷 - Descriptions of luxurious interior decorations, with '椒壁' (pepper walls) and '兰帷' (orchid curtains) evoking a sense of opulence.
  • 十女子 - Refers to the ten women worshipped in the temple, likely deities or spirits.
  • 翠羽明珰 - Descriptions of ornate accessories, with '翠羽' (jade feathers) and '明珰' (bright pendants) symbolizing beauty and wealth.
  • 姮娥 - The Chinese moon goddess, often associated with the story of Chang'e.
  • 青溪小姑子 - Reference to a figure from Chinese folklore, often associated with chastity and purity.
  • 广寒宫 - The Chinese name for the moon palace, the residence of the moon goddess.

Proper Nouns

  • 十姨庙 - Temple dedicated to the Ten Aunts.
  • 杜曲西 - Location in the western part of Duzhi.
  • 阿娇 - A concubine of Emperor Wu of Han, symbolizing luxury and indulgence.
  • 姮娥 - The Chinese moon goddess, often associated with the story of Chang'e.
  • 青溪小姑子 - Figure from Chinese folklore, often associated with chastity and purity.
  • 广寒宫 - The Chinese name for the moon palace, the residence of the moon goddess.
